## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`**
**Explain why the torque–armature current (T–Iₐ) characteristic of a DC shunt motor is a straight line passing nearly through the origin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The torque developed in a DC motor is given by $T = K \Phi I_a$. In a **shunt motor**, the field winding is connected directly across the supply; hence the field current $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ is **constant**. The flux $\Phi$ produced is therefore also constant (assuming linear magnetic operation).

Substituting constant $\Phi$:
$$T = (K\Phi) \cdot I_a = \text{constant} \times I_a$$

This is a **straight line through the origin** with slope $K\Phi$. In practice, a small no-load armature current $I_{a0}$ flows to overcome friction, windage, and core losses, producing a small no-load torque $T_0 = K\Phi I_{a0}$. Hence the line has a **slight offset** but is essentially linear. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`**
**State and prove the condition for maximum power developed in a DC motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The mechanical power developed is:
$$P = T\omega = \frac{K\Phi(V - K\Phi\omega)}{R_a} \cdot \omega = \frac{K\Phi V}{R_a}\omega - \frac{(K\Phi)^2}{R_a}\omega^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $\omega$ and setting to zero:
$$\frac{dP}{d\omega} = \frac{K\Phi V}{R_a} - \frac{2(K\Phi)^2}{R_a}\omega = 0$$

$$\omega_{max\,P} = \frac{V}{2K\Phi} = \frac{\omega_0}{2}$$

Substituting back:
$$P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4R_a}$$

Thus, **maximum power occurs at half the no-load speed**, and its value is $V^2/(4R_a)$. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 — Choice A]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat circuit diagram and block diagram, describe the experimental procedure to obtain the **T–Iₐ, T–N, and P–N characteristics** of a DC shunt motor. List the instruments used and state the rating of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** From the following test data of a 220 V, 1500 rpm DC shunt motor with $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, plot the T–Iₐ, T–N, and P–N curves and determine (i) the no-load speed, (ii) the speed at maximum power, and (iii) the maximum power developed.

| $I_a$ (A) | 1.5 | 5.0 | 10.0 | 15.0 | 20.0 | 25.0 | 30.0 |
|-----------|------|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| $N$ (rpm) | 1540 | 1525 | 1505 | 1480 | 1450 | 1410 | 1360 |

Assume no-load torque $T_0 = 0.4$ N·m.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Circuit Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.3 above for schematic.) A separately excited DC shunt motor is connected to a 220 V DC source through:
- A **main switch** (SPST)
- An **ammeter (0–30 A)** in series with the armature
- A **voltmeter (0–300 V)** across the armature terminals
- A **rheostat (500 $\Omega$, 1 A)** in series with the shunt field winding

**Mechanical loading arrangement:** A **prony brake / rope brake** is mounted on a brake drum coupled to the motor shaft. Two spring balances ($W_1$ and $W_2$) are used to measure the net tangential force. Speed is measured with a **tachometer** (digital photo / magnetic pickup type).

**Procedure [4 marks]:**
1. **No-load test:** With the field rheostat set to minimum resistance (maximum field), start the motor. Apply rated voltage $V = 220$ V. Record the no-load readings: $V_0$, $I_{a0}$, $N_0$, and $T_0 = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot r$ from the brake.
2. **Load test:** Gradually increase the mechanical load on the prony brake (by tightening the belt or adding weights) in steps of 2–3 A.
3. For each load step, record: $V_L$, $I_a$, $N$ (tachometer), $W_1$ and $W_2$ (spring balances).
4. Calculate torque at each point: $T = (W_1 - W_2) \times r$ (where $r$ = effective brake-drum radius in metres).
5. Calculate power: $P = 2\pi N T / 60$.
6. Tabulate and plot the three characteristics.

**Instruments and Ratings [3 marks]:**

| Instrument | Range | Type | Accuracy Class |
|------------|-------|------|----------------|
| Voltmeter | 0–300 V | Moving iron / M.I. | $\pm$1 % |
| Ammeter (Armature) | 0–30 A | M.I. | $\pm$1 % |
| Ammeter (Field) | 0–2 A | M.I. | $\pm$1 % |
| Tachometer | 0–2000 rpm | Digital | $\pm$0.5 % |
| Spring balance | 0–10 kgf / 0–50 N | Dial type | $\pm$0.5 % |
| Rheostat (Field) | 500 $\Omega$, 1 A | Wire-wound | — |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Calculate torque and power for each row:**

For each row, $T = K\Phi I_a - T_0$ where $K\Phi$ is to be found.

**Step 1a — Determine $K\Phi$ from the no-load condition:**

At no-load, $I_{a0} = 1.5$ A, $N_0 = 1540$ rpm, $T_0 = 0.4$ N·m.

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \times 1540}{60} = 161.22 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$K\Phi = \frac{T_0}{I_{a0}} = \frac{0.4}{1.5} = 0.267 \text{ N·m/A}$$

**Step 1b — Verify using back-EMF at no-load:**

$$E_b = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - 1.5 \times 0.5 = 219.25 \text{ V}$$

$$K\Phi = \frac{E_b}{\omega_0} = \frac{219.25}{161.22} = 1.360 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

> **Discrepancy note:** The two values of $K\Phi$ differ because $T_0$ given is **mechanical shaft torque** (already net of rotational losses), whereas the back-EMF value gives the **electromagnetic** $K\Phi$. We use the **electromagnetic** $K\Phi = 1.360$ V·s/rad for electromagnetic torque, or recompute as needed. For the purpose of this KTU-style answer, we will use the back-EMF value.

**Step 2 — Calculate $T$, $\omega$, $P$ for each row:**

Using $T = K\Phi(I_a - I_{a0})$ with $K\Phi = 1.360$:

| $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | $\omega$ (rad/s) | $T = 1.360(I_a - 1.5)$ (N·m) | $P = T\omega$ (W) |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| 1.5       | 1540      | 161.22           | 0.00                       | 0.0                |
| 5.0       | 1525      | 159.70           | 4.76                       | 760.2              |
| 10.0      | 1505      | 157.59           | 11.56                      | 1821.7             |
| 15.0      | 1480      | 154.97           | 18.36                      | 2845.7             |
| 20.0      | 1450      | 151.84           | 25.16                      | 3820.3             |
| 25.0      | 1410      | 147.65           | 31.96                      | 4718.6             |
| 30.0      | 1360      | 142.41           | 38.76                      | 5519.7             |

**Step 3 — Read off the characteristics [final values for the answer]:**

**(i) No-load speed:**
$$N_0 \approx 1540 \text{ rpm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = 161.22 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Speed at maximum power:** From the P–N curve, peak occurs near:
$$\omega_{max\,P} = \frac{\omega_0}{2} \approx 80.6 \text{ rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{max\,P} \approx 770 \text{ rpm}$$

(In the practical range of our data, the power is still rising; the curve is monotonic in the operating region because we never approach the stall condition.)

**(iii) Maximum developed power (theoretical, from the parabola):**
$$P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4R_a} = \frac{220^2}{4 \times 0.5} = \frac{48400}{2} = 24{,}200 \text{ W} = 24.2 \text{ kW}$$

This occurs at the **stall point** theoretically; in practice the motor is protected from reaching this by a **circuit breaker / overcurrent relay**.

---

### **Question 4 — Alternative Choice (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **T–N** and **P–N** relations for a DC shunt motor. Show that the speed at which maximum power is developed is **half the no-load speed**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 220 V DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$. At no-load, the motor draws 4 A from the supply and runs at 1400 rpm. Find (i) the no-load armature current, (ii) the flux per pole in terms of $K$, (iii) the developed torque at a load current of 50 A, and (iv) the speed at this load.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Derivation of T–N [3 marks]:**

From $E_b = V - I_a R_a = K \Phi \omega$:
$$I_a = \frac{V - K\Phi\omega}{R_a}$$

Substitute into $T = K\Phi I_a$:
$$T = \frac{K\Phi V}{R_a} - \frac{(K\Phi)^2}{R_a}\omega$$

This is a linear relation between $T$ and $\omega$ with negative slope $-(K\Phi)^2/R_a$.

**Derivation of P–N [2 marks]:**
$$P = T\omega = \frac{K\Phi V}{R_a}\omega - \frac{(K\Phi)^2}{R_a}\omega^2$$

**Condition for maximum power [2 marks]:**
$$\frac{dP}{d\omega} = \frac{K\Phi V}{R_a} - \frac{2(K\Phi)^2}{R_a}\omega = 0$$

$$\omega_{max\,P} = \frac{V}{2K\Phi} = \frac{\omega_0}{2}$$

Hence, **maximum power is developed at half the no-load speed**. ∎

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$, $I_L = 4$ A (no-load), $N_0 = 1400$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Field current at no-load:**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — No-load armature current [Answer (i)]:**
$$I_{a0} = I_L - I_{sh} = 4 - 2 = 2.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Back-EMF at no-load:**
$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - 2.0 \times 0.3 = 220 - 0.6 = 219.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — No-load angular velocity:**
$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi N_0}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1400}{60} = 146.61 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Flux in terms of $K$ [Answer (ii)]:**
$$K\Phi = \frac{E_{b0}}{\omega_0} = \frac{219.4}{146.61} = 1.496 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

$$\boxed{K\Phi = 1.496 \text{ Wb (in SI)}}$$

**Step 6 — At load, $I_a = 50$ A; developed torque [Answer (iii)]:**
$$T = K\Phi \cdot I_a = 1.496 \times 50 = 74.81 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Caveat (KTU standard):** This is the **developed (electromagnetic) torque** $T_{dev}$. The **shaft torque** $T_{sh} = T_{dev} - T_0$ where $T_0$ is the rotational loss torque. If the problem says "developed torque," we use $T = K\Phi I_a$ directly.

**Step 7 — Speed at this load [Answer (iv)]:**

Back-EMF at load:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 50 \times 0.3 = 220 - 15 = 205 \text{ V}$$

Speed:
$$N = N_0 \times \frac{E_b}{E_{b0}} = 1400 \times \frac{205}{219.4} = 1400 \times 0.9344 = 1308 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N = 1308 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 8 — Speed regulation:**
$$\text{Reg.} = \frac{1400 - 1308}{1308} \times 100 = 7.03\ \%$$

> This confirms that the DC shunt motor has good (low) speed regulation — a key characteristic.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this experiment (KTU valuation pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the no-load offset** ($T_0$, $I_{a0}$) on the T–Iₐ plot — the line does **not** pass through the origin in practice. **−1 mark** if missed.
> 2. **Confusing developed torque with shaft torque.** The prony brake measures *shaft* torque, but the equation $T = K\Phi I_a$ gives *developed* torque. Always clarify in your answer. **−2 marks** if mixed.
> 3. **Wrong unit of speed in rad/s conversion.** Use $2\pi N / 60$, NOT $2\pi N$. **−1 mark** per occurrence.
> 4. **Not plotting all three characteristics on the same graph sheet.** KTU lab record requires **all three** plots — T–Iₐ, T–N, and P–N — clearly labelled with axes, units, and a smooth curve. **−3 marks** if missing.
> 5. **Using $T = K\Phi I_a$ to "measure" torque.** This is a circular calculation. The **prony brake measurement** ($T = (W_1 - W_2)r$) is the **experimental** value; the equation is only for verification.
> 6. **Drawing the P–N curve as a straight line.** It must be a **parabola** peaking at $N_0/2$. **−2 marks** if linear.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert the brake-drum radius from mm to m** in $T = (W_1 - W_2)r$. **−1 mark** for unit error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Lab Exam**
>
> - [ ] DC shunt motor T–Iₐ characteristic is a **straight line** with slope $K\Phi$ and offset $T_0$ on the y-axis.
> - [ ] DC shunt motor T–N characteristic is a **slightly drooping straight line** (≈5–10 % speed drop from NL to FL).
> - [ ] DC shunt motor P–N characteristic is an **inverted parabola** peaking at **half the no-load speed** ($N_0/2$).
> - [ ] **Two fundamental equations** (memorize these):
>   - $T = K\Phi I_a$
>   - $E_b = K\Phi\omega$
> - [ ] **KVL on armature:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — always start here for any motor problem.
> - [ ] **Torque is measured** with a prony brake: $T = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot r$ (N·m).
> - [ ] **Power is computed** as $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (Watts) using measured values.
> - [ ] **No-load speed:** $\omega_0 = V/(K\Phi)$ (the y-intercept on the T–N plot).
> - [ ] **Stall torque:** $T_{stall} = K\Phi V / R_a$ (the y-intercept on the T–N plot at $N=0$).
> - [ ] **Maximum power:** $P_{max} = V^2 / (4R_a)$ at $N = N_0/2$.
> - [ ] **Instruments** required: Voltmeter, Ammeter (×2), Tachometer, Spring balances, Field rheostat, Fuse/MCB.
> - [ ] **Rheostat in field** controls speed *above* base speed; **rheostat in armature** controls speed *below* base speed (this is a common viva question).
> - [ ] **Why is the shunt motor called a "constant-speed motor"?** Because the T–N line has a small negative slope — speed changes very little with load.
> - [ ] **Lab safety:** Never start a DC motor without the field winding energized (open-field starting causes dangerously high speed → mechanical destruction).
> - [ ] **Most common viva questions:**
>   1. *Why does the shunt motor's P–N curve peak at $N_0/2$?* → Mathematical condition $dP/d\omega = 0$.
>   2. *What is the difference between developed torque and shaft torque?* → Shaft torque = Developed torque − Rotational-loss torque.
>   3. *How is speed measured in the lab?* → Tachometer (digital/optical) or stroboscope.
>   4. *Why use a starting resistor in a DC motor?* → To limit the inrush current at start (when $E_b = 0$, $I_a = V/R_a$ can be very high).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip (Last-Minute Strategy):**
> 1. Always begin your answer with the **KVL equation** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — examiners look for it in the first line.
> 2. When asked to **plot characteristics**, use a **graph sheet** with proper title, axis labels, units, and a smooth curve (not zig-zag).
> 3. Always mention the **no-load offset** explicitly — it is worth 1–2 marks and is a frequent KTU favourite.
> 4. In **numerical problems**, show **two decimal places** for $\omega$ and **three significant figures** for $N$ to be safe.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using ultrasonic sensors, stepper/servo motors and infrared sensors, develop a line follower robot that will follow a trajectory, avoiding obstacles on its path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL607 - PCCPL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Using ultrasonic sensors, stepper/servo motors and infrared sensors, develop a line follower robot that will follow a trajectory, avoiding obstacles on its path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Line Follower Robot with Obstacle Avoidance** is an autonomous, embedded-system-based electromechanical agent designed to navigate a predefined visual trajectory (typically a dark line on a light surface, or vice-versa) using **infrared (IR) reflectance sensors**, while simultaneously detecting and circumventing static or dynamic obstacles on its path using **ultrasonic time-of-flight (ToF) ranging sensors**. The locomotion is achieved through precision actuators—either **stepper motors** for open-loop precise angular displacement or **servo motors** for closed-loop positional control—driven by a microcontroller executing a real-time decision algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCPL607 – Module Experiment 2):**
> The experiment tests the integration of **three sensor modalities** (IR + Ultrasonic) and **two actuator classes** (Stepper/Servo) into a single reactive agent governed by a **finite state machine (FSM)** based control loop. KTU examiners expect both the **hardware interfacing schematic** and the **algorithmic control flow** in the answer script.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Man with a White Cane"

Imagine a person walking inside a corridor painted with a black line on a white floor:

- **His feet (IR sensors):** Two small devices pointed at the floor continuously ask, "Am I standing on the black line, or have I drifted to the white side?" Based on the answer, his brain nudges him left or right.
- **His white cane (ultrasonic sensor):** He waves it ahead and listens for the echo. If an echo returns within a critical time, he knows a wall or chair is in front; he stops, scans sideways, and walks around it.
- **His leg muscles (motors):** Stepper motors move his legs in *exact, repeatable steps* (good for precise turns), while servo motors act like a steering wheel that rotates to a specific angle and holds there (good for smooth steering).
- **His brain (microcontroller):** A small computer (Arduino/ATmega328P) reads all these senses, makes a decision within **milliseconds**, and commands the muscles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Critical Performance Metrics** (Bolded for KTU Valuation):
> 1. **Line Tracking Latency:** $\le 50$ ms
> 2. **Obstacle Detection Range:** **$2\text{ cm}$ to $400\text{ cm}$** (HC-SR04)
> 3. **Servo PWM Refresh Rate:** **$50$ Hz** (i.e., **$20$ ms** period)

## 1.3 System-Level Block Components

| Subsystem | KTU-Standard Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing – Line | **IR Reflectance Sensor Array (TCRT5000)** | Detects line via reflected IR intensity |
| Sensing – Range | **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Transducer** | Measures distance via $343$ m/s sound waves |
| Actuation – Drive | **NEMA-17 Stepper / SG90 Servo / BO Motor** | Provides linear/angular motion |
| Actuation – Steering | **SG90 Micro Servo** | Rotates sensor mount or wheel axis |
| Driver Layer | **L293D / L298N / DRV8825** | Current amplification & signal buffering |
| Control Layer | **ATmega328P (Arduino Uno/Nano)** | Executes sensing-decision-actuation loop |
| Power Layer | **7.4 V Li-ion / 9 V battery pack** | Supplies motor and logic power |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IR Sensor Subsystem — Working Principle

An IR reflectance sensor (TCRT5000) is composed of:
- An **IR LED** that emits light at **$\lambda \approx 940$ nm** (invisible to human eye).
- A **phototransistor / photodiode** that conducts proportionally to the *reflected* IR intensity.

The reflected intensity obeys the **inverse-square law**, but in practice the sensor is mounted at a fixed $1\text{–}2$ cm from the ground. The discriminating property is **diffuse reflectance**:

$$\rho_{\text{black}} \ll \rho_{\text{white}}$$

> A **white surface** reflects $\sim 80\text{–}90\%$ of incident IR (high phototransistor current $\Rightarrow$ LOW digital output).
> A **black surface** absorbs $\sim 95\%$ of incident IR (low phototransistor current $\Rightarrow$ HIGH digital output).

The output is fed to a **comparator (LM393)** that produces a clean digital logic level—**0 V (LOW)** on white, **+5 V (HIGH)** on black (or vice-versa, configurable).

### 2.1.1 Multi-Sensor Array Geometry

A robust line follower uses **3 to 5 IR sensors** mounted in a row, slightly forward of the wheel axis, with a typical inter-sensor pitch of $p = 1.5$ cm (matched to the line width $w$ of $1.5\text{–}3$ cm):

| Sensor Position | Condition Detected | Robot Action |
|---|---|---|
| Left | On black | Drift right (reduce left motor PWM) |
| Center | On black | Move forward (equal PWM) |
| Right | On black | Drift left (reduce right motor PWM) |
| None | Off-track | Search mode: rotate in last-known direction |
| All three | Intersection / T-junction | Decision branch (turn/predefined) |

## 2.2 Ultrasonic Sensor Subsystem — Time-of-Flight Principle

The HC-SR04 has four pins: **VCC, TRIG, ECHO, GND**. Operating sequence:

1. MCU sends a **$\ge 10 \text{ μs}$ HIGH pulse** on TRIG.
2. The transducer emits **eight $40$ kHz ultrasonic bursts** (above human hearing).
3. Sound travels to the obstacle, reflects, returns.
4. ECHO pin goes HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time $t_{\text{echo}}$.

The fundamental governing equation is the **sonic ToF equation**:

$$d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$$

where $v_{\text{sound}}$ is the speed of sound in air. The factor of $2$ accounts for round-trip travel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed of Sound Correction:** The speed of sound varies with air temperature. The standard reference is:
> $$v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s, where } T \text{ is in °C)}$$
> At **$T = 20°C$**, $v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \times 20 = \mathbf{343 \text{ m/s}}$.
> KTU examiners often test whether students apply the **division by 2** for round-trip.

### 2.2.1 Time-to-Distance Conversion Substituting $v = 343$ m/s

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{0.0343 \text{ cm/μs} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}(\text{μs})}{2} = 0.01715 \cdot t_{\text{echo}}(\text{μs})$$

Equivalently, the digital conversion factor commonly used in Arduino code is:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{t_{\text{echo}}}{58}$$

where $58 = \dfrac{2}{0.0343}$, or in inches:

$$d_{\text{in}} = \frac{t_{\text{echo}}}{148}$$

## 2.3 Servo Motor — PWM Positional Control

A hobby servo (e.g., **SG90**) is a closed-loop DC motor with a built-in potentiometer for position feedback. It is controlled by a **PWM signal of fixed $20$ ms period**, with the pulse width encoding the target angle:

| Pulse Width | Output Shaft Angle |
|---|---|
| $1.0$ ms | $0°$ (full counter-clockwise) |
| $1.5$ ms | $90°$ (center) |
| $2.0$ ms | $180°$ (full clockwise) |

The linear interpolation formula for any desired angle $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$ is:

$$t_{\text{pulse}}(\text{ms}) = 1.0 + \frac{\theta}{180} \times 1.0 = 1.0 + \frac{\theta}{180}$$

The required PWM duty cycle on a $50$ Hz signal:

$$D_{\%} = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{20} \times 100$$

## 2.4 Stepper Motor — Open-Loop Step Angle Control

A stepper motor rotates in discrete **step angles** $\theta_s$. The common **NEMA-17** has $\theta_s = 1.8°$, meaning:

$$N_{\text{steps/rev}} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps}$$

With **microstepping** ($m = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32$):

$$\theta_{\text{micro}} = \frac{1.8°}{m}$$

The rotational speed $\omega$ relates to step frequency $f$:

$$\omega = f \cdot \theta_s = \frac{2\pi f}{N_{\text{steps/rev}}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** If a stepper motor needs to rotate a wheel of radius $r$ to cover a distance $L$, the required number of steps is:
> $$N_{\text{steps}} = \frac{L}{2\pi r} \cdot N_{\text{steps/rev}} \cdot m$$

## 2.5 Robot Linear Velocity

The chassis contains two drive wheels of radius $r$, separated by axle length $D$. The linear velocity at each wheel is:

$$v = \omega \cdot r$$

For **differential steering**, the angular velocity of the robot body is:

$$\omega_{\text{body}} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{D}$$

A **zero-radius turn** (pivot) occurs when $v_L = -v_R$, giving $\omega_{\text{body}} = \frac{2 v_R}{D}$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Distance From Echo Pulse Width

**Given:** The HC-SR04 returns an ECHO HIGH pulse of duration $t_{\text{echo}}$ measured in microseconds. The ambient temperature is $T = 25°$C. Find the distance in cm to the obstacle.

### Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound at the given temperature

We start from the linear temperature dependence:

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$$

Substituting $T = 25$:

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6 \times 25$$

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 15$$

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 346 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to cm/μs for unit consistency

Since $1 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ cm}$ and $1 \text{ s} = 10^6 \text{ μs}$:

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 346 \text{ m/s} \times \frac{100 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ m}} \times \frac{1 \text{ s}}{10^6 \text{ μs}}$$

$$v_{\text{sound}} = \frac{34600}{10^6} \text{ cm/μs}$$

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 0.0346 \text{ cm/μs}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the round-trip time-of-flight equation

The ToF equation yields one-way distance:

$$d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$d = \frac{0.0346 \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$$

$$d = 0.0173 \cdot t_{\text{echo}} \text{ (in cm)}$$

### Step 4 — Equivalent division form

Dividing numerator and denominator by $0.0173$:

$$d = \frac{t_{\text{echo}}}{1/0.0173} = \frac{t_{\text{echo}}}{57.8} \approx \frac{t_{\text{echo}}}{58}$$

> **Final Compact Formula:** $\quad d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{echo}}(\mu s)}{58}$

**Verification:** If $t_{\text{echo}} = 5800 \text{ μs}$, then $d = 5800 / 58 = 100$ cm $= 1$ m. Physical check: $t = 2d/v = (2 \times 1) / 346 = 0.00578$ s $= 5780$ μs $\approx 5800$ μs. ✔ Consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steps Required to Traverse a Given Distance

**Given:** A NEMA-17 stepper motor (1.8°/step) drives a wheel of radius $r = 3$ cm through a **$1:1$ direct drive** (no gearbox). Microstepping is set to $m = 1$. Find the number of steps required to traverse $L = 94.2$ cm.

### Step 1 — Compute steps per revolution

$$N_{\text{steps/rev}} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the wheel's circumference

$$C = 2\pi r = 2 \times \pi \times 3 = 6\pi \text{ cm}$$

Numerically: $C = 6 \times 3.14159 = 18.8495$ cm per revolution.

### Step 3 — Compute the number of full revolutions required

$$n_{\text{rev}} = \frac{L}{C} = \frac{94.2}{18.8495}$$

$$n_{\text{rev}} = 5.0 \text{ revolutions (exact)}$$

### Step 4 — Compute total steps

$$N_{\text{steps}} = n_{\text{rev}} \times N_{\text{steps/rev}} \times m$$

$$N_{\text{steps}} = 5 \times 200 \times 1 = 1000 \text{ steps}$$

> **Final Answer:** The microcontroller must output **1000 step pulses** to the DRV8825 driver.

**Cross-check using combined formula:**

$$N_{\text{steps}} = \frac{L}{2\pi r} \cdot N_{\text{steps/rev}} \cdot m = \frac{94.2}{18.8495} \times 200 \times 1 = 1000 \text{ steps} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Servo Pulse Width for $135°$ Rotation

**Given:** A SG90 servo is commanded to rotate to $\theta = 135°$. Compute the pulse width and the duty cycle on a $50$ Hz PWM.

### Step 1 — Apply the linear interpolation formula

$$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \frac{\theta}{180} = 1.0 + \frac{135}{180}$$

$$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + 0.75 = 1.75 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the duty cycle

The PWM period at $50$ Hz is $T = 20$ ms:

$$D_{\%} = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T} \times 100 = \frac{1.75}{20} \times 100$$

$$D_{\%} = 8.75\%$$

> **Result:** Generate a PWM of **$8.75\%$ duty cycle** on a **$50$ Hz** signal to position the servo at $135°$. On Arduino's `analogWrite()`, this maps to an 8-bit OCR value of:
> $$N_{\text{Timer}} = \frac{8.75}{100} \times 255 \approx 22$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Differential Steering Yaw Rate for a $90°$ Turn

**Given:** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 4$ cm and axle length $D = 15$ cm. The right wheel moves forward at $v_R = +10$ cm/s and the left wheel is held stationary ($v_L = 0$). Compute (a) the time to complete a $90°$ turn, and (b) the turning radius $R$.

### Part (a) — Yaw rate calculation

The yaw rate of the robot body:

$$\omega_{\text{body}} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{D} = \frac{10 - 0}{15} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to degrees per second:

$$\omega_{\text{body}} = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{180°}{\pi} = 38.197°/\text{s}$$

### Part (b) — Time for a $90°$ rotation

$$t_{\text{turn}} = \frac{90°}{38.197°/\text{s}} = 2.356 \text{ s}$$

### Part (c) — Turning radius (Instantaneous Center of Curvature)

The ICC lies on the axle extension, at distance $R$ from the midpoint:

$$R = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$

Since $v_L = 0$:

$$R = \frac{15}{2} \cdot \frac{10 + 0}{10 - 0} = 7.5 \text{ cm}$$

> **Conclusion:** The robot pivots in a circle of radius **$7.5$ cm**, completing a $90°$ arc in **$2.356$ seconds**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Decision Logic

| Left IR | Center IR | Right IR | Ultrasonic $d$ | Robot Action |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 0 (white) | 1 (black) | 0 (white) | $d > 20$ cm | **FORWARD** (equal PWM) |
| 1 (black) | 0 (white) | 0 (white) | $d > 20$ cm | **SHARP LEFT** (reverse right, forward left) |
| 0 (white) | 0 (white) | 1 (black) | $d > 20$ cm | **SHARP RIGHT** |
| 1 (black) | 1 (black) | 0 (white) | $d > 20$ cm | **SOFT LEFT** (reduce left PWM) |
| 0 (white) | 1 (black) | 1 (black) | $d > 20$ cm | **SOFT RIGHT** |
| 1 (black) | 1 (black) | 1 (black) | $d > 20$ cm | **CROSSROAD** (stop, signal, then turn) |
| x | x | x | $d \le 20$ cm | **OBSTACLE MODE** (stop + scan with servo) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Convention Used:** `1 = line detected (black)`, `0 = floor (white)`. This must match the comparator threshold in the LM393 module. KTU examiners will deduct marks for inconsistent polarity.

---

## 3.6 Combined Finite State Machine (FSM) for Autonomous Navigation

The robot is governed by a **Mealy FSM** with three primary states:

- **State $S_0$ (LINE_FOLLOW):** Default state; uses IR array for lateral correction.
- **State $S_1$ (OBSTACLE_DETECTED):** Triggered when $d \le 20$ cm; interrupts line following.
- **State $S_2$ (AVOID):** Servo rotates ultrasonic sensor to scan left/right, picks the direction with maximum clearance, executes a $90°$ turn, advances past the obstacle, then returns to $S_0$.

The transition conditions are:

$$\delta(S_0, d \le 20) = S_1$$
$$\delta(S_1, \text{scan\_complete}) = S_2$$
$$\delta(S_2, \text{cleared}) = S_0$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCPL607]`
**Explain the working principle of an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor. Derive the formula used to compute distance from the echo pulse width.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) The HC-SR04 emits a $40$ kHz ultrasonic burst when triggered by a $\ge 10 \text{ μs}$ HIGH pulse on its TRIG pin. The burst travels to the obstacle, reflects, and returns to the transducer. The ECHO pin goes HIGH for a duration $t_{\text{echo}}$ proportional to the round-trip travel time.
- (1 Mark) Applying $d = v \cdot t / 2$ with $v_{\text{sound}} = 343$ m/s at $20°$C and converting to cm/μs:
$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{0.0343 \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$$
- (1 Mark) Simplifying: $d_{\text{cm}} = t_{\text{echo}} / 58$. The factor $2$ in the denominator accounts for the sound travelling to the obstacle and back (round trip).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCPL607]`
**Differentiate between a stepper motor and a servo motor. State one application of each in a line follower robot.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Stepper motor** rotates in fixed angular increments (step angle, e.g., $1.8°$ for NEMA-17) using open-loop control—no feedback sensor required. Its position is determined by the number of pulse signals sent to the driver.
- (1 Mark) **Servo motor** uses closed-loop control—an internal potentiometer provides positional feedback. The shaft is held at a specific angle based on the PWM pulse width ($1\text{ ms}$ to $2\text{ ms}$ for $0°$ to $180°$).
- (1 Mark) **Applications:** A stepper motor is used for *wheel drive* where precise linear displacement is needed (e.g., traversing exactly $30$ cm during obstacle avoidance). A servo motor is used for *steering* or for *rotating the ultrasonic sensor mount* to scan left/right for obstacle clearance.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 + 7**. Two independent choices (A and B) are provided as per ESE pattern.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCPL607]`

**Design a line follower robot with obstacle avoidance capability. Your answer must include:**
**(a)** A **block diagram** showing all major subsystems, with a clear description of **three sensors** (one IR array, one ultrasonic) and **two motor types** (stepper/servo) used. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A **flowchart/algorithm** (with pseudocode) that describes the **finite state machine** governing the robot, including obstacle detection threshold, scanning logic, and re-acquisition of the line. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram and Component Description (7 Marks):**

The system comprises the following sub-blocks:

1. **Power Subsystem (1 Mark):** A $7.4$ V, $1500$ mAh Li-ion battery pack supplies raw power. A buck regulator (LM2596) provides regulated $5$ V for logic and a separate $5$ V/separate rail for motors to prevent noise coupling.
2. **Sensor Subsystem (2 Marks):**
   - **IR Reflectance Array (TCRT5000 ×3):** Mounted on the front bumper with $1.5$ cm pitch. Each contains an IR LED ($\lambda = 940$ nm) and a phototransistor with an LM393 comparator. Outputs a digital HIGH when the floor is black (line detected) and LOW when white.
   - **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor:** Mounted on top of an **SG90 micro-servo** so it can physically rotate $\pm 90°$ to scan left and right. Range $2\text{ cm}$–$400\text{ cm}$ with $\pm 3$ mm accuracy. Triggered every $100$ ms.
3. **Actuator Subsystem (2 Marks):**
   - **Two NEMA-17 Stepper Motors** (one per wheel) driven by **A4988/DRV8825** microstepping drivers, providing open-loop precise displacement during obstacle-avoidance maneuvers.
   - **One SG90 Servo** for rotating the ultrasonic sensor head.
4. **Controller (1 Mark):** An **ATmega328P** (Arduino Uno/Nano) running at $16$ MHz. It executes a $50$ Hz main loop reading sensors, computing state transitions, and outputting PWM/step signals.
5. **Indicator/Communication (1 Mark):** A piezo buzzer and LEDs for debugging; an optional HC-05 Bluetooth module for remote override.

A labeled functional block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**(b) Algorithm and State Machine (7 Marks):**

**High-Level FSM (3 Marks):**

The robot operates in three states—$S_0$ (LINE_FOLLOW), $S_1$ (OBSTACLE_SCAN), $S_2$ (AVOID_AND_REACQUIRE). Transitions are triggered by sensor thresholds.

**Pseudocode Implementation (4 Marks):**

```
ALGORITHM LineFollowerWithObstacleAvoid
    CONST THRESHOLD = 20    // cm, obstacle trigger
    CONST SERVO_LEFT = 0
    CONST SERVO_CENTER = 90
    CONST SERVO_RIGHT = 180
    CONST AVOID_DIST = 30   // cm, forward after turning

BEGIN
    Initialize pins, PWM 50Hz on servo pin
    Calibrate IR sensors over black and white
    Set initial state = LINE_FOLLOW

    WHILE (system_powered == TRUE) DO
        SELECT current_state

        CASE LINE_FOLLOW:
            L = digitalRead(IR_LEFT)
            C = digitalRead(IR_CENTER)
            R = digitalRead(IR_RIGHT)
            d = readUltrasonic()  // in cm

            IF (d <= THRESHOLD) THEN
                state = OBSTACLE_SCAN
                stopMotors()
            ELSE IF (C == 1) THEN
                moveForward(PWM = 180)
            ELSE IF (L == 1 AND C == 0) THEN
                turnLeft(PWM_L = 100, PWM_R = 200)
            ELSE IF (R == 1 AND C == 0) THEN
                turnRight(PWM_L = 200, PWM_R = 100)
            ELSE
                searchLine()  // all white; rotate in place
            END IF

        CASE OBSTACLE_SCAN:
            servoWrite(SERVO_LEFT)
            DELAY 200 ms
            d_left = readUltrasonic()

            servoWrite(SERVO_RIGHT)
            DELAY 200 ms
            d_right = readUltrasonic()

            servoWrite(SERVO_CENTER)
            IF (d_left > d_right) THEN
                turnLeft90()
            ELSE
                turnRight90()
            END IF
            state = AVOID_AND_REACQUIRE

        CASE AVOID_AND_REACQUIRE:
            moveForwardDistance(AVOID_DIST)  // 30 cm
            turnBack()  // 90° opposite direction
            WHILE (digitalRead(IR_CENTER) == 0) DO
                moveForward(PWM = 150)  // hunt for line
            END WHILE
            state = LINE_FOLLOW

        END SELECT
    END WHILE
END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ultrasonic ToF equation (sound travels to *and* from the obstacle). This is the #1 error; costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Mixing sensor polarity** (e.g., saying HIGH = white) without verifying the comparator threshold—deducted **1 mark** in component description.
> 3. **Omitting the state transition condition** in the FSM diagram (e.g., showing only states without arrows/conditions)—deducted **1.5 marks**.
> 4. **Wrong servo period** (using $5$ ms or $10$ ms instead of $20$ ms) in the PWM derivation—costs **1 mark** in the formula sheet question.
> 5. **Not stating microstepping mode** when computing NEMA-17 step counts—deducted **0.5 mark** if $m > 1$ is used without declaration.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCPL607]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain how a **$3$-sensor IR array** is interfaced to an Arduino. Describe the comparator action of the LM393 module and derive the relationship between reflectance and digital output. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A **NEMA-17 stepper motor** ($200$ steps/rev, microstepping $m = 4$) drives a wheel of radius $r = 3.5$ cm directly. Compute: **(i)** the **number of steps** required to cover a distance of $1.1$ m; **(ii)** the **rotational speed** in RPM if the step pulse frequency is $f = 1600$ Hz; **(iii)** the **linear velocity** of the wheel in cm/s. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) IR Array Interfacing (7 Marks):**

1. **Hardware Description (2 Marks):** Each TCRT5000 module has 4 pins: **VCC, GND, DO (digital out), AO (analog out)**. Three such modules are mounted on a front bumper, $1.5$ cm apart, wired in parallel to the $5$ V and GND rails, with their DO pins connected to Arduino digital pins `D2`, `D3`, `D4` (left, center, right).
2. **Comparator Action (3 Marks):** The onboard LM393 comparator has its inverting input tied to a potentiometer (sets the threshold voltage $V_{\text{th}}$), and the non-inverting input connected to the phototransistor's collector via a voltage divider. When the phototransistor conducts heavily (white floor), $V_{\text{photo}} > V_{\text{th}}$ and the comparator output swings to **LOW** (saturated transistor). When the phototransistor is starved of light (black floor), $V_{\text{photo}} < V_{\text{th}}$ and the output swings to **HIGH** (open-collector pulled up by $10$ kΩ).
3. **Reflectance Relationship (2 Marks):** The reflected intensity obeys the diffuse reflectance equation $I_r = I_0 \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\theta / d^2$, where $\rho$ is the surface reflectance ($\rho_{\text{white}} \approx 0.85$, $\rho_{\text{black}} \approx 0.05$). The phototransistor collector current $I_C \propto I_r$, so:
$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{CC}} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{\text{CE}}(I_r)}$$
- On white: $R_{\text{CE}}$ small $\Rightarrow V_{\text{out}} \approx 0$ (LOW).
- On black: $R_{\text{CE}}$ large $\Rightarrow V_{\text{out}} \approx V_{\text{CC}}$ (HIGH).

**(b) Stepper Motor Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Number of steps to cover $L = 1.1$ m = $110$ cm (3 Marks):**

First, compute steps per revolution with microstepping:

$$N_{\text{eff}} = N_{\text{steps/rev}} \times m = 200 \times 4 = 800 \text{ microsteps/rev}$$

Circumference of the wheel:

$$C = 2\pi r = 2 \times \pi \times 3.5 = 7\pi \approx 21.991 \text{ cm/rev}$$

Number of revolutions:

$$n_{\text{rev}} = \frac{L}{C} = \frac{110}{21.991} = 5.0018 \approx 5 \text{ revolutions}$$

Total microsteps:

$$N_{\text{steps}} = 5.0018 \times 800 \approx 4001 \text{ microsteps}$$

**(ii) Rotational speed in RPM (2 Marks):**

$$\omega = f \times \theta_{\text{micro}} = 1600 \times \frac{1.8°}{4} = 1600 \times 0.45° = 720°/\text{s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_{\text{RPM}} = \frac{720°/\text{s}}{360°/\text{rev}} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iii) Linear velocity in cm/s (2 Marks):**

$$v = \omega \cdot r = \frac{2\pi N_{\text{RPM}}}{60} \times r$$

$$v = \frac{2\pi \times 120}{60} \times 3.5 = 4\pi \times 3.5 = 14\pi \approx 43.98 \text{ cm/s}$$

> **Final Results:**
> (i) $\sim 4001$ microsteps
> (ii) $120$ RPM
> (iii) $\approx 44$ cm/s

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **The ultrasonic ToF formula requires division by 2** — the sound travels to the obstacle and returns. This is the most common mark-loss point.
- **Speed of sound is temperature dependent:** $v = 331 + 0.6T$ m/s. At $20°$C it is **$343$ m/s**.
- **The HC-SR04 digital conversion factors** are $58$ for cm and $148$ for inches — memorize both.
- **A servo motor requires $50$ Hz PWM (period $= 20$ ms)** with pulse width $1.0\text{ ms} \leftrightarrow 0°$, $1.5\text{ ms} \leftrightarrow 90°$, $2.0\text{ ms} \leftrightarrow 180°$.
- **A stepper motor's step angle** is $\theta_s$ (e.g., $1.8°$ for NEMA-17). With microstepping $m$, the effective microstep angle is $\theta_s / m$.
- **The formula for steps to cover a distance $L$:** $N_{\text{steps}} = \dfrac{L}{2\pi r} \times N_{\text{steps/rev}} \times m$.
- **Differential steering yaw rate:** $\omega_{\text{body}} = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{D}$ — differential of the two wheel speeds over the axle length.
- **The IR sensor logic level depends on the comparator threshold** — always state the polarity convention explicitly in the answer.
- **Obstacle detection threshold** in a typical experiment is **$d \le 20$ cm**.
- **The robot's autonomous behavior is best modeled as a Finite State Machine** with three states: LINE_FOLLOW, OBSTACLE_SCAN, AVOID_AND_REACQUIRE. State transitions must include the *triggering condition*.
- **HC-SR04 trigger pulse must be $\ge 10$ μs**, and you must wait at least $60$ ms between successive measurements to avoid echo collisions.
- **The robot uses open-loop stepper control for obstacle avoidance maneuvers** (precise $30$ cm advance) and **closed-loop servo control for sensor steering** (precise scan angles).
- **KTU loves numeric problems** on NEMA-17 stepper calculations and ultrasonic distance conversion — practice at least three of each.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Warning:**
> - **Never** mix up the units. If the question gives distance in cm and the formula yields meters, the answer will be marked zero even if the algebra is correct.
> - **Always** specify the temperature when quoting $v_{\text{sound}}$. If omitted, expect a **0.5 mark deduction**.
> - **Always** draw the FSM with three components: **state name, transition arrow, transition condition**. A bare circle with a name inside is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using DC servo/stepper motors, implement a pick and place SCARA robot arm in 3D plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL607 - PCCPL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Using DC servo/stepper motors, implement a pick and place SCARA robot arm in 3D plane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)** is a cylindrical-coordinate, four-axis (or three-axis) robotic manipulator characterized by its rigid, vertically-stiff structure combined with horizontal compliance. It is specifically engineered for high-speed, high-precision **pick-and-place** operations, assembly tasks, and palletizing in a horizontal plane.

In the context of this experiment (KTU Module Experiment 2), the SCARA arm is implemented using **DC servo motors** (for closed-loop positional control of revolute joints) and/or **stepper motors** (for open-loop discrete angular positioning), and is programmed to traverse a 3D working envelope to pick objects from a source location and place them at a target location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SCARA manipulators belong to the *Type 3 (Vertical Prismatic)* joint arrangement as per the Japanese Industrial Robot Association (JIRA) classification, and exhibit **R (Revolute) – P (Prismatic) – P (Prismatic)** or **R – R – P** configurations in the DH parameter framework.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Waiter Carrying a Tray"

Imagine a human waiter carrying a tray of glasses across a dining hall:

| Human Action | SCARA Robot Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Rotating the shoulder horizontally | Revolute Joint 1 (driven by Servo/Stepper) |
| Extending the elbow horizontally | Revolute Joint 2 (prismatic-like rigid link) |
| Lifting/lowering the tray vertically | Prismatic Joint 3 (Z-axis) |
| Tilting the wrist to grip the glass | Roll/Pitch joint at end-effector |

The key intuition is that a SCARA robot is **stiff in the vertical (Z) direction** (the waiter does not let the tray sag or bounce up/down) but **compliant in the horizontal (X-Y) plane** (allowing rapid, smooth swiveling motions). This is precisely why it is the industry gold-standard for PCB assembly, screw driving, and pick-and-place.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering metrics must be noted for a SCARA pick-and-place implementation:

- **Workspace Radius:** Typically **100 mm to 1000 mm** (industrial) / **150 mm to 300 mm** (educational kits like the KTU lab setup).
- **Repeatability:** ± **0.02 mm to ± 0.05 mm** for servo-driven SCARAs.
- **Stepper Resolution:** **1.8° per full step** (200 steps/rev) or **0.9° per full step** (400 steps/rev) with standard NEMA motors.
- **Servo PWM Pulse Width:** Standard hobby servo: **1 ms (0°) to 2 ms (180°)** with a period of **20 ms (50 Hz)**.
- **Gripper Actuation Force:** Typically **5 N to 30 N** for educational pneumatic/electric grippers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Compliance:** In robotics, *compliance* refers to the degree to which a mechanism yields (deforms) under an applied force. A SCARA arm is *selectively compliant* — stiff vertically, compliant horizontally.

## 1.4 Objective of the Experiment

The lab experiment mandates that the student must:
1. **Identify the mechanical structure** of the 4-DOF (or 3-DOF) SCARA arm.
2. **Interface DC servo / stepper motors** to a microcontroller (Arduino UNO / 8051 / ARM Cortex / PLC).
3. **Generate coordinated joint trajectories** to move from a pick coordinate $(x_p, y_p, z_p)$ to a place coordinate $(x_q, y_q, z_q)$ in the 3D workspace.
4. **Actuate the end-effector (gripper)** to grasp, lift, transport, lower, and release the object.
5. **Validate** the operation via simulation (MATLAB/SolidWorks) and/or hardware execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of the SCARA Arm

A SCARA arm is composed of four functional subsystems:

1. **Base & Shoulder Module (Joint 1):** Houses the first revolute joint driven by a DC servo or stepper motor. Provides rotation about the **Z-axis** (yaw).
2. **Link 1 (Upper Arm):** A rigid horizontal link of length **L₁**, typically between **80 mm and 200 mm** for lab kits.
3. **Elbow Module (Joint 2):** The second revolute joint, coaxial with Joint 1 in motion plane. Driven by an independent motor.
4. **Link 2 (Forearm):** A rigid horizontal link of length **L₂**.
5. **Vertical Prismatic Joint (Joint 3):** A linear slide along **Z-axis** driven by a lead-screw or rack-and-pinion coupled to a stepper/servo.
6. **End-Effector (Gripper):** A two-finger (or three-finger) gripper, often servo-driven or pneumatic, with a small "wrist roll" for object orientation.

## 2.2 Motor Selection Logic — Why Servo vs. Stepper?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:** Examiners frequently ask students to justify motor selection for each joint. Memorize the table below.

| Parameter | DC Servo Motor | Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Loop** | Closed-loop (with rotary encoder feedback) | Open-loop (no feedback) |
| **Positioning Method** | PWM pulse width (1 ms–2 ms) | Discrete step pulses (PWM/Step/Dir) |
| **Holding Torque at Standstill** | High (active feedback maintains position) | High (detent torque holds position) |
| **Speed Range** | High RPM, smooth at all speeds | Loses torque at high RPM |
| **Risk of Missed Steps** | None (encoder corrects) | High under overload |
| **Cost** | Higher (requires encoder + driver) | Lower |
| **Best Joint Assignment (KTU Typical)** | Joint 1 (shoulder), Joint 3 (vertical lift) | Joint 2 (elbow), or both for low-cost kits |
| **Heat Generation** | Lower (only draws current when correcting) | Higher (always energized holding position) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — SCARA Kinematics

The following equations are the **mandatory toolkit** for the lab viva and university exam. All symbols use SI units (mm, rad, s).

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forward Kinematics — Position** | $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | X-coordinate of end-effector |
| 2 | **Forward Kinematics — Position** | $y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | Y-coordinate of end-effector |
| 3 | **Forward Kinematics — Vertical** | $z = d_3$ | Vertical position (prismatic joint) |
| 4 | **End-Effector Orientation** | $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ | Roll angle about Z-axis |
| 5 | **Inverse Kinematics — $\theta_2$** | $\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$ | Elbow angle (elbow-up / elbow-down) |
| 6 | **Inverse Kinematics — $\theta_1$** | $\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)$ | Shoulder angle |
| 7 | **Inverse Kinematics — $d_3$** | $d_3 = z$ | Direct prismatic mapping |
| 8 | **Stepper Angular Resolution** | $\theta_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{\text{steps/rev}} \cdot \mu}$ | $\mu$ = microstepping factor (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32) |
| 9 | **Servo PWM Conversion** | $T_{\text{pulse}} = 1\text{ ms} + \dfrac{\theta_{\text{desired}}}{180°} \times 1\text{ ms}$ | Converts angle to pulse width |
| 10 | **Link Reach Constraint** | $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq L_1 + L_2$ | Workspace boundary |
| 11 | **Trajectory Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{r \cdot \omega}{60}$ | Linear velocity from joint angular velocity $\omega$ (RPM) and radius $r$ |
| 12 | **Torque (Static Load)** | $\tau = m \cdot g \cdot r$ | Holding torque needed at joint for mass $m$ at radius $r$ |
| 13 | **Gripper PWM** | Servo at 0° = open; Servo at 90° = closed (typical) | End-effector actuation |
| 14 | **Joint Space Velocity** | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Inverse Jacobian mapping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "arctan2" and not "arctan"?** The two-argument function $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$ correctly identifies the quadrant of the angle, returning values in $(-\pi, \pi]$. Using $\arctan(y/x)$ alone would fail for quadrants II and III — a common student error.

## 2.4 The 3D Plane Concept — A Subtle Clarification

A SCARA's *primary* motion is planar (X-Y), but it is termed a **3D plane robot** because the third axis (Z) provides vertical motion, giving it a **cylindrical working envelope**. The term "3D plane" in the experiment title refers to this cylindrical workspace — **not** a full 6-DOF articulated arm envelope.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Electronics Manufacturing** | Soldering, SMD component placement on PCBs |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Sorting pills, vial capping |
| **Food & Beverage** | Packing chocolates, biscuits, bottles |
| **Automotive** | Screw-driving, sub-assembly insertion |
| **KTU Lab Curriculum** | Teaching forward/inverse kinematics, trajectory planning, motor control, embedded programming |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Forward Kinematics for a 2-Link SCARA

We consider a planar 2-link arm in the X-Y plane. **Link 1** has length $L_1$ and is oriented at angle $\theta_1$ from the X-axis. **Link 2** has length $L_2$ and is oriented at an angle $\theta_2$ **relative to Link 1** (i.e., absolute angle = $\theta_1 + \theta_2$).

### Step 1 — Position of Joint 2 (Elbow)

The position of the elbow joint $E$ in the base frame is:

$$
E = (L_1 \cos\theta_1,\ L_1 \sin\theta_1)
$$

This is the projection of Link 1 onto the X and Y axes. The text-row logic: $L_1$ is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with adjacent side on X-axis ($L_1 \cos\theta_1$) and opposite side on Y-axis ($L_1 \sin\theta_1$).

### Step 2 — Position of End-Effector (Tip)

The end-effector position $P = (x, y)$ is the sum of the elbow position and the vector from elbow to tip:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ is expanded using the trigonometric addition identity:

$$
\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1 \sin\theta_2
$$

Substituting this and the analogous $\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ identity yields the **equivalent expanded form** often required in KTU derivations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_1 - (L_2 \sin\theta_2)\sin\theta_1 \\
y &= (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)\sin\theta_1 + (L_2 \sin\theta_2)\cos\theta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the homogeneous transformation matrix formulation using the Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters.

### Step 3 — Vertical Position

For the SCARA, the third joint is a prismatic joint along Z. Hence:

$$
z = d_3
$$

where $d_3$ is the displacement of the prismatic joint from its zero reference. No rotation couples to the X-Y plane.

### Step 4 — End-Effector Orientation

The end-effector roll (rotation about Z) is the sum of the two revolute joint angles:

$$
\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Inverse Kinematics

**Given** a desired end-effector position $(x_d, y_d, z_d)$ and desired orientation $\phi_d$, **find** $(\theta_1, \theta_2, d_3)$.

### Step 1 — Direct Vertical Mapping

The prismatic joint decouples from the planar problem, so:

$$
d_3 = z_d
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $r^2 = x_d^2 + y_d^2$

The squared distance from the base to the target is:

$$
r^2 = x_d^2 + y_d^2
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Cosine Rule (Law of Cosines)

In the triangle formed by the base, elbow, and tip, the side opposite to $\theta_2$ is the link from base to tip of length $r$. Applying the cosine rule:

$$
r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 - 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(180° - \theta_2)
$$

Since $\cos(180° - \theta_2) = -\cos\theta_2$, this simplifies to:

$$
r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

Solving for $\cos\theta_2$:

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}
$$

Hence:

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

The **$+$** sign corresponds to the **elbow-down** configuration; the **$-$** sign corresponds to **elbow-up**. Both are valid; the choice depends on obstacle avoidance and joint-limit constraints.

### Step 4 — Solve for $\theta_1$

Let $k_1 = L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2$ and $k_2 = L_2 \sin\theta_2$. The forward kinematics equations can be rewritten as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_d &= k_1 \cos\theta_1 - k_2 \sin\theta_1 \\
y_d &= k_1 \sin\theta_1 + k_2 \cos\theta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard inverse derivation:

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y_d, x_d) - \operatorname{atan2}(k_2, k_1)
$$

This produces a unique $\theta_1 \in (-\pi, \pi]$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

> **Problem:** A SCARA arm has $L_1 = 200$ mm, $L_2 = 150$ mm. The gripper must reach a target at $(x, y, z) = (250, 100, 50)$ mm. Compute $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (elbow-up).

### Solution

$$
r^2 = 250^2 + 100^2 = 62500 + 10000 = 72500 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
r = \sqrt{72500} \approx 269.26 \text{ mm}
$$

**Reachability check:** $|L_1 - L_2| = 50$ mm and $L_1 + L_2 = 350$ mm. Since $50 \leq 269.26 \leq 350$ — target is reachable.

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{72500 - 200^2 - 150^2}{2 \cdot 200 \cdot 150} = \dfrac{72500 - 40000 - 22500}{60000} = \dfrac{10000}{60000} = 0.1667
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \arccos(0.1667) \approx 80.41° \quad \text{(elbow-up sign: use } -80.41°\text{)}
$$

For elbow-up, we take $\theta_2 = -80.41°$ (i.e., $k_2 = L_2 \sin\theta_2 < 0$):

$$
k_1 = 200 + 150 \cdot \cos(-80.41°) = 200 + 150 \cdot 0.1667 = 200 + 25.0 = 225.0
$$

$$
k_2 = 150 \cdot \sin(-80.41°) = 150 \cdot (-0.9860) = -147.9
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(100, 250) - \operatorname{atan2}(-147.9, 225.0)
$$

$$
\operatorname{atan2}(100, 250) \approx 21.80° \quad ; \quad \operatorname{atan2}(-147.9, 225.0) \approx -33.33°
$$

$$
\theta_1 \approx 21.80° - (-33.33°) = 55.13°
$$

**Verification** (substituting back into forward kinematics):

$$
x_{\text{calc}} = 200 \cos(55.13°) + 150 \cos(55.13° - 80.41°) = 200(0.5721) + 150 \cos(-25.28°)
$$

$$
x_{\text{calc}} \approx 114.42 + 150(0.9038) \approx 114.42 + 135.57 = 249.99 \text{ mm} \approx 250 \text{ mm } \checkmark
$$

$$
y_{\text{calc}} = 200 \sin(55.13°) + 150 \sin(-25.28°) = 200(0.8202) + 150(-0.4272) \approx 164.04 - 64.08 = 99.96 \text{ mm} \approx 100 \text{ mm } \checkmark
$$

**Final Joint Commands:**

$$
\boxed{\theta_1 \approx 55.13°,\quad \theta_2 \approx -80.41°,\quad d_3 = 50 \text{ mm}}
$$

## 3.4 Stepper Motor Command Derivation

For a stepper motor with **$N = 200$ steps/rev** and **microstepping $\mu = 2$** (½ step):

$$
\theta_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{360°}{200 \times 2} = 0.9° \text{ per microstep}
$$

Number of steps for $\theta_1 = 55.13°$:

$$
n_1 = \dfrac{55.13°}{0.9°} \approx 61.26 \rightarrow 62 \text{ steps (rounded up)}
$$

Number of steps for $\theta_2 = 80.41°$ (magnitude):

$$
n_2 = \dfrac{80.41°}{0.9°} \approx 89.34 \rightarrow 90 \text{ steps}
$$

## 3.5 Servo PWM Pulse Derivation

For $\theta_1 = 55.13°$ (assume 0°–180° range):

$$
T_{\text{pulse}} = 1000\,\mu\text{s} + \dfrac{55.13}{180} \times 1000\,\mu\text{s} = 1000 + 306.3 = 1306.3\,\mu\text{s} \approx 1.31 \text{ ms}
$$

This pulse is sent every 20 ms (50 Hz refresh) to maintain the position.

## 3.6 Trajectory Planning (Linear Interpolation)

To move smoothly from $(x_p, y_p, z_p)$ to $(x_q, y_q, z_q)$ in $N$ discrete steps, the interpolated position at step $i$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(i) &= x_p + \dfrac{i}{N}(x_q - x_p) \\
y(i) &= y_p + \dfrac{i}{N}(y_q - y_p) \\
z(i) &= z_p + \dfrac{i}{N}(z_q - z_p)
\end{aligned}
$$

for $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N$. At each $i$, the inverse kinematics is solved to get $(\theta_1(i), \theta_2(i), d_3(i))$ which is then sent to the motors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Mechanical Architecture of the SCARA Arm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base and Power Supply] --> B[Revolute Joint 1 Shoulder Servo Stepper]
    B --> C[Link 1 L1 Rigid Horizontal]
    C --> D[Revolute Joint 2 Elbow Servo Stepper]
    D --> E[Link 2 L2 Rigid Horizontal]
    E --> F[Prismatic Joint 3 Vertical Lead Screw Stepper]
    F --> G[Wrist Roll Joint Optional Servo]
    G --> H[End Effector Two Finger Gripper Servo]
    H --> I[Workpiece Pick and Place Object]

    J[Microcontroller Arduino 8051 ARM] --> B
    J --> D
    J --> F
    J --> G
    J --> H

    K[Trajectory and IK Algorithm] --> J
    L[Power Driver IC L293D ULN2003 A4988] --> B
    L --> D
    L --> F
    J --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Control Flow of the Pick-and-Place Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Program] --> S2[Initialize Motors and Sensors]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**Q:** Define a SCARA robot. Why is it preferred for pick-and-place operations in a horizontal plane?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)** is a four-axis robotic manipulator with two parallel revolute joints providing X-Y motion in the horizontal plane and one prismatic joint providing vertical (Z) motion. It is **selectively compliant** — meaning it is compliant (allows some deflection) in the horizontal plane to absorb minor misalignments during insertion tasks, but rigid in the vertical direction to maintain a constant tool height.
>
> It is preferred for **pick-and-place** because:
> 1. Its **rigid vertical axis** ensures precise Z-positioning, avoiding dropping or crushing the object.
> 2. Its **fast planar motion** (high accelerations up to 10 g) allows high cycle rates (50–200 picks/min).
> 3. Its **simple inverse kinematics** (decoupled planar + linear) simplifies control.
> 4. Its **compact cylindrical envelope** fits easily into assembly workstations.
>
> *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "selective compliance" explanation, 1 mark for the four reasons summarized.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q:** Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control in the context of stepper vs. servo motors used in SCARA joints.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Stepper Motor (Open-loop) | DC Servo Motor (Closed-loop) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Feedback | None | Rotary encoder on shaft |
> | Position error correction | Not possible — if a step is missed, it is lost forever | Encoder feedback continuously corrects the error |
> | Holding torque at rest | Provided by detent torque; motor remains energized and heats up | Provided only as needed; PID controller stops sending correction pulses |
> | Typical use in SCARA | Low-cost educational kits, low-payload joints | Industrial SCARAs, high-precision joints, joints with high inertia loads |
>
> *(Examiner allocates: 1.5 marks for the conceptual distinction, 1.5 marks for the implication in SCARA use.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reachability check** $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq L_1 + L_2$ before solving inverse kinematics → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $\arctan$ instead of $\operatorname{atan2}$** → loses 1 mark; examiner expects the quadrant-aware function.
> 3. **Ignoring elbow-up vs. elbow-down ambiguity** in $\theta_2$ → loses 1 mark; you must mention both solutions exist.
> 4. **Forgetting the gripper actuation step** in the pick-and-place sequence → loses 1 mark; pick-and-place is *not* complete without gripper open/close.
> 5. **Confusing stepper "step angle" with "full revolution"** — students often forget to divide by microstepping factor $\mu$ → loses 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [Choice Option 1 — KTU Exam Pattern]

**Q:** A SCARA manipulator has $L_1 = 300$ mm and $L_2 = 200$ mm. The robot is driven by a stepper motor with 200 steps/revolution at the shoulder (no microstepping) and a DC servo motor at the elbow.
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Compute the joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to position the end-effector at $(x, y) = (350, 150)$ mm. Use the **elbow-up** configuration.
**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Describe in detail the step-by-step pick-and-place control sequence (with a flowchart) to move an object from point A = (200, 100, 30) mm to point B = (400, 250, 30) mm. Include the role of both motors and the gripper servo.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Reachability check:**

$$
r = \sqrt{350^2 + 150^2} = \sqrt{122500 + 22500} = \sqrt{145000} \approx 380.79 \text{ mm}
$$

Bounds: $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = 100$ mm, $L_1 + L_2 = 500$ mm. Since $100 \leq 380.79 \leq 500$, target is **reachable**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos\theta_2$:**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{145000 - 300^2 - 200^2}{2 \cdot 300 \cdot 200} = \dfrac{145000 - 90000 - 40000}{120000} = \dfrac{15000}{120000} = 0.125
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\theta_2$ (elbow-up → negative):**

$$
\theta_2 = -\arccos(0.125) \approx -82.82° \quad \text{or in radians} \approx -1.4455 \text{ rad}
$$

*(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for correct numerical value with correct sign convention)*

**Step 4 — Compute intermediate values:**

$$
k_1 = L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 300 + 200(0.125) = 300 + 25 = 325 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
k_2 = L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 200 \cdot \sin(-82.82°) = 200 \cdot (-0.9922) = -198.44 \text{ mm}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Compute $\theta_1$:**

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(150, 350) - \operatorname{atan2}(-198.44, 325)
$$

$$
\operatorname{atan2}(150, 350) \approx 23.20° \quad ; \quad \operatorname{atan2}(-198.44, 325) \approx -31.41°
$$

$$
\theta_1 \approx 23.20° - (-31.41°) = 54.61°
$$

**Step 6 — Stepper pulse count for shoulder:**

For a 200 steps/rev motor with $\mu = 1$:

$$
\theta_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{360°}{200} = 1.8° \text{ per step}
$$

$$
n_1 = \dfrac{54.61°}{1.8°} \approx 30.34 \rightarrow 31 \text{ step pulses (rounded)}
$$

**Step 7 — Servo PWM for elbow:**

$$
T_{\text{pulse}} = 1000\,\mu\text{s} + \dfrac{\vert -82.82 \vert}{180°} \times 1000\,\mu\text{s} \approx 1000 + 460 = 1460\,\mu\text{s} = 1.46 \text{ ms}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\theta_1 \approx 54.61° \text{ (31 step pulses)},\quad \theta_2 \approx -82.82° \text{ (servo PWM 1.46 ms)}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The complete pick-and-place sequence:

1. **System Initialization** *(0.5 mark):*
   - Power on the microcontroller (Arduino).
   - Initialize all motor driver pins as outputs.
   - Attach gripper servo and move to "open" position (0°).

2. **Homing Routine** *(0.5 mark):*
   - Drive shoulder stepper towards home limit switch.
   - Drive elbow servo to its 0° reference pulse (1.0 ms).
   - Drive vertical stepper upwards until Z-home limit switch triggers.
   - Reset all joint counters to zero.

3. **Move to Pick Position — Planar Phase** *(1.5 marks):*
   - Use inverse kinematics to compute $(\theta_{1A}, \theta_{2A})$ for $(x_A, y_A) = (200, 100)$.
   - Generate coordinated step pulses for shoulder and synchronized servo command for elbow to reach the target.
   - Use a **linear interpolation** of $N = 50$ intermediate points for smooth motion.

4. **Vertical Descent** *(0.5 mark):*
   - Drive the Z-axis stepper to descend to $z = 30$ mm (grasp height).
   - Total step count = $(H_{\text{home}} - 30) / \text{lead\_screw\_pitch} \times 200$ steps/rev.

5. **Gripper Actuation** *(0.5 mark):*
   - Send PWM of 1.5 ms (90°) to close the gripper servo.
   - Wait 500 ms for mechanical stability.

6. **Vertical Lift (Safe Transit Height)** *(0.5 mark):*
   - Drive Z-axis stepper upward to $z_{\text{safe}} = 80$ mm (clear of obstacles).

7. **Move to Place Position — Planar Phase** *(1.5 marks):*
   - Recompute inverse kinematics for $(x_B, y_B) = (400, 250)$.
   - Drive shoulder stepper and elbow servo to $(\theta_{1B}, \theta_{2B})$ using linear interpolation.

8. **Vertical Descent at Place** *(0.5 mark):*
   - Lower Z-axis back to $z = 30$ mm.

9. **Gripper Release** *(0.5 mark):*
   - Send PWM of 1.0 ms (0°) to open the gripper.
   - Wait 500 ms for object release.

10. **Return to Home** *(0.5 mark):*
    - Raise Z-axis to safe height.
    - Return all joints to home position.
    - Signal "Cycle Complete" (e.g., LED blink or LCD message).

*Examiner notes: 1 mark reserved for the flowchart structure; 1 mark for the gripper actuation step being explicitly mentioned.*

### Question B (14 Marks) [Choice Option 2 — Alternative]

**Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a SCARA-based pick-and-place robot driven by DC servo and stepper motors.
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the mechanical structure, degrees of freedom, and the role of each actuator.
**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Derive the forward kinematics equations for the SCARA manipulator and explain how the joint angles are commanded using PWM and step pulses.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Mechanical Structure (3 marks):**
A SCARA manipulator consists of:
- A **stationary base** mounted on a worktable.
- **Joint 1 (Shoulder):** A revolute joint with a vertical axis of rotation (Z-axis), driven by a DC servo motor. The servo receives a PWM signal (1–2 ms) corresponding to the desired angle.
- **Link 1:** A rigid horizontal arm of length $L_1$, typically made of aluminum or carbon fiber for stiffness-to-weight ratio.
- **Joint 2 (Elbow):** A second revolute joint, parallel to Joint 1, driven by a stepper motor. The stepper receives discrete step pulses to rotate by exact angular increments.
- **Link 2:** A rigid horizontal forearm of length $L_2$.
- **Joint 3 (Wrist/Vertical):** A prismatic joint providing vertical motion, driven by a stepper motor coupled to a lead screw or rack-and-pinion.
- **End-effector:** A two-finger pneumatic or servo gripper, often with a wrist-roll joint for object orientation.

**Degrees of Freedom (2 marks):**
The standard SCARA has **4 DOF**:
1. $\theta_1$ — rotation of Link 1 (shoulder).
2. $\theta_2$ — rotation of Link 2 (elbow).
3. $d_3$ — vertical translation (Z-axis).
4. $\theta_4$ — end-effector roll (optional, for symmetric objects).

This gives a **cylindrical working envelope** of radius $L_1 + L_2$ and height equal to the Z-axis stroke.

**Role of Each Actuator (2 marks):**
- **DC Servo at Joint 1:** Provides high-torque, closed-loop positioning of the shoulder. Encoder feedback ensures $\theta_1$ is exact even under load.
- **Stepper at Joint 2:** Provides open-loop, repeatable angular positioning of the elbow. Cheaper than a servo, suitable for lower inertia demands.
- **Stepper at Joint 3:** Provides precise vertical motion of the prismatic joint via a lead screw. Open-loop is acceptable because gravity load is predictable.
- **Servo at Gripper:** Provides the open/close actuation of the end-effector fingers with a simple 0°/90° PWM command.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Forward Kinematics Derivation (4 marks):**

Using the convention described in Section 3.1, the position of the end-effector is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
z &= d_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation steps:**
- Position of elbow $(L_1 \cos\theta_1, L_1 \sin\theta_1)$.
- Add Link 2 vector at absolute angle $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.
- Z is independently set by the prismatic joint.

**PWM Command Generation for Servo (1.5 marks):**
- For a desired $\theta_1$, the microcontroller computes $T_{\text{pulse}} = 1000 + (\theta_1 / 180) \times 1000$ microseconds.
- This pulse is sent to the servo every 20 ms using `Servo.write()` (Arduino) or hardware PWM peripheral.
- The servo's internal PID loop drives the motor until the encoder matches the commanded position.

**Step Pulse Generation for Stepper (1.5 marks):**
- For a desired $\theta_2$, the step count is $n_2 = \theta_2 \times N_{\text{steps/rev}} \times \mu / 360°$.
- The microcontroller toggles the STEP pin of the A4988/DRV8825 driver at a controlled frequency $f = v_{\text{step}}$.
- The DIR pin sets the rotation direction (HIGH = CCW, LOW = CW).
- Acceleration/deceleration ramps are used to avoid missed steps at high speeds.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Block — Print and Re-Read Before the Exam**

- ✅ **SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** — compliant horizontally, rigid vertically.
- ✅ **Configuration = R-R-P** (revolute-revolute-prismatic) with optional 4th axis for wrist roll.
- ✅ **Forward Kinematics:** $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$; $y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$; $z = d_3$.
- ✅ **Inverse Kinematics:** Use **Law of Cosines** to get $\theta_2$, then **atan2** to get $\theta_1$.
- ✅ **Always check reachability:** $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq L_1 + L_2$.
- ✅ **Two valid solutions** for $\theta_2$ → elbow-up and elbow-down — choose based on joint limits/obstacles.
- ✅ **Servo PWM formula:** $T_{\text{pulse}} = 1\text{ ms} + (\theta / 180°) \times 1\text{ ms}$, refreshed at 50 Hz.
- ✅ **Stepper step formula:** $n = \theta \times N \times \mu / 360°$, where $N$ = steps/rev, $\mu$ = microstepping.
- ✅ **Standard stepper:** $N = 200$ (1.8°/step); with $\mu = 16$, resolution becomes **0.1125°/microstep**.
- ✅ **Pick-and-place sequence:** Home → Move to pick → Lower → Close gripper → Raise → Move to place → Lower → Open gripper → Raise → Return home.
- ✅ **DC Servo = closed-loop** (uses encoder); **Stepper = open-loop** (no feedback, can lose steps).
- ✅ **Workspace:** Cylindrical envelope of radius $(L_1 + L_2)$ and height $d_{3,\text{max}}$.
- ✅ **Applications:** PCB assembly, screw-driving, packaging, pill sorting, lab automation.
- ✅ **Key advantages:** High speed (up to 10 g acceleration), high repeatability (±0.02 mm), simple kinematics.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Limited to quasi-planar tasks; cannot easily reach around 3D obstacles like a 6-DOF arm.
